papers.title: Risk-Aware Rulebooks for Multi-Objective Trajectory Evaluation under Uncertainty
papers.abstract: We present a risk-aware formalism for evaluating system trajectories in the presence of uncertain interactions between the system and its environment. The proposed formalism supports reasoning under uncertainty and systematically handles complex relationships among requirements and objectives, including hierarchical priorities and non-comparability. Rather than treating the environment as exogenous noise, we explicitly model how each system trajectory influences the environment and evaluate trajectories under the resulting distribution of environment responses. We prove that the formalism induces a preorder on the set of system trajectories, ensuring consistency and preventing cyclic preferences. Finally, we illustrate the approach with an autonomous driving example that demonstrates how the formalism enhances explainability by clarifying the rationale behind trajectory selection.
papers.pdf_first_page_text:
Risk-Aware Rulebooks for Multi-Objective Trajectory Evaluation under
Uncertainty
Tichakorn Wongpiromsarn1
Abstract— We present a risk-aware formalism for evaluating
system trajectories in the presence of uncertain interactions be-
tween the system and its environment. The proposed formalism
supports reasoning under uncertainty and systematically han-
dles complex relationships among requirements and objectives,
including hierarchical priorities and non-comparability. Rather
than treating the environment as exogenous noise, we explicitly
model how each system trajectory influences the environment
and evaluate trajectories under the resulting distribution of
environment responses. We prove that the formalism induces a
preorder on the set of system trajectories, ensuring consistency
and preventing cyclic preferences. Finally, we illustrate the ap-
proach with an autonomous driving example that demonstrates
how the formalism enhances explainability by clarifying the
rationale behind trajectory selection.
I. INTRODUCTION
Safety-critical autonomous systems, such as autonomous
vehicles, must satisfy multiple safety requirements, including
avoiding collisions, adhering to local driving norms, and
obeying traffic laws. At the same time, these systems need
to optimize various objectives, such as minimizing travel
time and maximizing passenger comfort. However, these
requirements and objectives often conflict, and in some cases,
it may not even be possible to satisfy all safety requirements
simultaneously [1]. For example, on a highway with a 65
mph speed limit, where most humans drive at 80 mph, an
autonomous vehicle faces a dilemma between adhering to
the speed limit or adapting to the flow of traffic, with both
choices potentially increasing the risk of accidents. This
complexity necessitates a framework that supports formally
justifiable trade-offs, where certain requirements may be
violated if necessary. While requirements such as those that
affect human safety are more important than others, other
requirements and objectives may not be comparable. More-
over, decision-making in these systems needs to take into
account uncertainties stemming from imperfect perception
and unstructured and unpredictable environments.
Variants of temporal logics have been employed as formal
specification languages to precisely define the requirements
of safety-critical systems [2]–[4]. Recent extensions, such
as Metric Temporal Logic [5], robust Linear-time Temporal
Logic [6], and Signal Temporal Logic (STL) [7]–[10] include
teraction by allowing the system trajectory to influence the
distribution of environmental behaviors. Each rule therefore
induces a trajectory-dependent random variable whose risk
is assessed using a user-specified risk measure and threshold.
This enables a unified treatment of expectation-based, worst-
case, and tail-risk criteria within a single preordered rule
structure. We prove that the proposed risk-aware rulebook
induces a preorder over the set of system trajectories. This
ensures consistency by preventing cyclic preferences (e.g.,
trajectory A is better than B, B is better than C, but C is better
than A); thus, ensuring that the notion of “optimal” trajectory
is well-defined. We further establish a connection between
safety and optimality under this formalism. In addition,
we show that optimal trajectories satisfy principled tradeoff
properties: if an alternative trajectory strictly improves upon
an optimal trajectory under one rule, then it must incur a
compensating deterioration under another rule that is not
lower in priority. In this sense, optimal trajectories are
rational as their selection is supported by transparent and
structurally consistent tradeoffs across competing objectives
under uncertainty. By making these tradeoffs explicit, our
formalism provides a basis for explainability: preferences
between trajectories can be traced to well-defined rule-
level comparisons governed by the rule preorder, clarifying
how uncertainty and partially prioritized objectives jointly
determine the selected trajectory.
II. PRELIMINARIES
Throughout the paper, we letR,R ≥0, andNdenote the set
of real, non-negative real, and natural numbers, respectively.
The expectation of a random variablefis denoted byE[f].
A. Rulebooks
This section provides an overview of a formalism called
“rulebooks” introduced in [30]. At the core of this framework
is the concept of ordering rules based on their relative
priority, which defines preference relations over a set of
possiblerealizations, denoted byΞ. We now provide precise
definitions of the foundational elements of this formalism.
Definition 1:Aruleis a functionr: Ξ→R ≥0 that
measures the degree of violation of its argument.
Ifr(x)< r(y), then the realizationyviolates the rulerto
a greater extent than doesx. In particular,r(x) = 0indicates
that a realizationxis fully compliant with the rule. Note that
the definition of the violation metric might be analytical,
“from first principles”, or be the result of a learning process.
Additionally, while we refer to this function as a “rule”, it is

papers.title: Dual-Interaction-Aware Cooperative Control Strategy for Alleviating Mixed Traffic Congestion
papers.abstract: As Intelligent Transportation System (ITS) develops, Connected and Automated Vehicles (CAVs) are expected to significantly reduce traffic congestion through cooperative strategies, such as in bottleneck areas. However, the uncertainty and diversity in the behaviors of Human-Driven Vehicles (HDVs) in mixed traffic environments present major challenges for CAV cooperation. This paper proposes a Dual-Interaction-Aware Cooperative Control (DIACC) strategy that enhances both local and global interaction perception within the Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework for Connected and Automated Vehicles (CAVs) in mixed traffic bottleneck scenarios. The DIACC strategy consists of three key innovations: 1) A Decentralized Interaction-Adaptive Decision-Making (D-IADM) module that enhances actor's local interaction perception by distinguishing CAV-CAV cooperative interactions from CAV-HDV observational interactions. 2) A Centralized Interaction-Enhanced Critic (C-IEC) that improves critic's global traffic understanding through interaction-aware value estimation, providing more accurate guidance for policy updates. 3) A reward design that employs softmin aggregation with temperature annealing to prioritize interaction-intensive scenarios in mixed traffic. Additionally, a lightweight Proactive Safety-based Action Refinement (PSAR) module applies rule-based corrections to accelerate training convergence. Experimental results demonstrate that DIACC significantly improves traffic efficiency and adaptability compared to rule-based and benchmark MARL models.

JOURNAL OF LATEX CLASS FILES, VOL. 14, NO. 8, AUGUST 2021 1
Dual-Interaction-Aware Cooperative Control
Strategy for Alleviating Mixed Traffic Congestion
Zhengxuan Liu, Student Member, IEEE, Yuxin Cai, Student Member, IEEE, Yijing Wang, Xiangkun
He, Senior Member, IEEE, Chen Lv, Senior Member, IEEE and Zhiqiang Zuo, Senior Member, IEEE
Abstract—As Intelligent Transportation System (ITS) develops,
Connected and Automated Vehicles (CA Vs) are expected to signif-
icantly reduce traffic congestion through cooperative strategies,
such as in bottleneck areas. However, the uncertainty and diver-
sity in the behaviors of Human-Driven Vehicles (HDVs) in mixed
traffic environments present major challenges for CA V coopera-
tion. This paper proposes a Dual-Interaction-Aware Cooperative
Control (DIACC) strategy that enhances both local and global
interaction perception within the Multi-Agent Reinforcement
Learning (MARL) framework for Connected and Automated
Vehicles (CA Vs) in mixed traffic bottleneck scenarios. The DIACC
strategy consists of three key innovations: 1) A Decentralized
Interaction-Adaptive Decision-Making (D-IADM) module that
enhances actor’s local interaction perception by distinguishing
CA V-CA V cooperative interactions from CA V-HDV observational
interactions. 2) A Centralized Interaction-Enhanced Critic (C-
IEC) that improves critic’s global traffic understanding through
interaction-aware value estimation, providing more accurate
guidance for policy updates. 3) A reward design that employs
softmin aggregation with temperature annealing to prioritize
interaction-intensive scenarios in mixed traffic. Additionally, a
lightweight Proactive Safety-based Action Refinement (PSAR)
module applies rule-based corrections to accelerate training
convergence. Experimental results demonstrate that DIACC sig-
nificantly improves traffic efficiency and adaptability compared
to rule-based and benchmark MARL models.
Index Terms—Mixed traffic, cooperative control, interac-
tion perception, multi-agent reinforcement learning, interaction-
intensive scenario.
I. I NTRODUCTION
W
ITH the rapid growth of vehicle ownership, traffic con-
gestion has become a critical challenge, and connected
and automated vehicles (CA Vs) have demonstrated significant
potential for improving traffic performance [1], [2]. However,
Human-Driven Vehicles (HDVs) will continue to coexist with
CA Vs for the foreseeable future [3]. Human drivers exhibit
diverse and unpredictable driving styles, posing a fundamental
challenge for CA Vs that must adapt to uncertain HDV behav-
iors while coordinating toward overall traffic optimization.
JOURNAL OF LATEX CLASS FILES, VOL. 14, NO. 8, AUGUST 2021 2
ments. However, single-agent RL algorithms have limitations
in designing effective cooperative strategies for CA Vs in mixed
traffic. They are more suitable for solving local control prob-
lems [9]. Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) offers
a more robust solution by facilitating coordination among
multiple agents through interaction and information sharing,
thereby improving the learning of cooperative strategies and
enhancing training efficiency. Consequently, MARL is an ap-
propriate and effective scheme for addressing the cooperative
control of CA Vs in the bottleneck scenarios discussed in
this paper. However, in the decentralized MARL framework,
cooperation between CA Vs is restricted to local observations,
limiting the improvement of global traffic efficiency. In fact,
the influence of vehicle interactions on traffic performance is a
global problem, while existing MARL methods focus on local
vehicle interactions and lack consideration for global traffic
dynamics.
As discussed above, vehicle interaction is a critical factor
influencing traffic performance in bottleneck scenarios, how-
ever, it remains inadequately addressed in existing approaches.
To bridge this gap, we propose a Dual-Interaction-Aware Co-
operative Control (DIACC) strategy that enhances interaction
perception from both local and global perspectives within the
MARL framework. The main contributions of this paper are
as follows:
• To address the actor’s inability to distinguish between
heterogeneous vehicle interactions (i.e., CA Vs, HDVs), a
Decentralized Interaction-Adaptive Decision-Making (D-
IADM) module is proposed. The core design employs
separate graph attention networks to differentiate CA V-
CA V cooperative interactions (incorporating previous-
timestep decision feedback) from CA V-HDV observa-
tional interactions that rely solely on historical trajecto-
ries. Experimental results demonstrate improved training
stability, higher traffic efficiency, and reduced safety-
critical events.
• Centralized Interaction-Enhanced Critic (C-IEC) is de-
vised to enable the critic to capture how vehicle interac-
tions influence global traffic dynamics during training.
This provides a more accurate value estimation that
guides D-IADM toward cooperative strategies account-
ing for system-level traffic dynamics. The performance
advantage is particularly pronounced in high-density sce-
narios.
papers.abstract_for_prompt: As Intelligent Transportation System (ITS) develops, Connected and Automated Vehicles (CAVs) are expected to significantly reduce traffic congestion through cooperative strategies, such as in bottleneck areas. However, the uncertainty and diversity in the behaviors of Human-Driven Vehicles (HDVs) in mixed traffic environments present major challenges for CAV cooperation. This paper proposes a Dual-Interaction-Aware Cooperative Control (DIACC) strategy that enhances both local and global interact
papers.topic_rationale: Cooperative CAV control in traffic; vehicle domain, no articulated manipulation or whole-body control.

executive_summary: Dexterous and long-horizon manipulation see notable advances, from privileged tactile sim-to-real transfer (PTLD) and massive bimanual grasp datasets (UltraDexGrasp) to memory- and horizon-scaled diffusion policies (VPWEM, SeedPolicy).

Motion planning progresses on multiple fronts, including GPU-accelerated dynamics-aware planning for high-DoF robots (cuRoboV2), multi-IK RRT* for joint space (Many-RRT*), and graph/learning-informed samplers (GAIDE) and uncertainty-aware OBCA.

Mobile and humanoid manipulation capabilities are expanded through risk-aware RL for mobile manipulation, omnidirectional LiDAR-based whole-body control (Omni-Manip), and physics-aware whole-body VLA frameworks (PhysiFlow).

Vision-language(-action) models are increasingly central in control stacks, enabling contact-tolerant planning (DCT), tri-system long-horizon manipulation (Critic in the Loop), neuro-symbolic skill growth from feedback (MEMO), and mechanistic control of VLA internal features.

Memory and representation learning for manipulation get systematic treatment via benchmarks and architectures (RoboMME, VPWEM, HyperMVP), improving robustness to occlusions, non-Markovian tasks, and geometric complexity.

Digital twins and simulation tools for manipulation, from Gaussian-splatting-based twins (GaussTwin) to CT-driven surgical planning and industrial textile sorting, illustrate a trend toward tightly coupled real-to-sim planning loops.

institution_matches.title: PTLD: Sim-to-real Privileged Tactile Latent Distillation for Dexterous Manipulation
institution_matches.institution: Carnegie Mellon University; UC Berkeley; University of Washington
institution_matches.overview: Introduces PTLD, a sim-to-real framework that bypasses tactile simulation by using privileged real-world tactile sensing to train a tactile state estimator. This estimator augments proprioceptive policies trained in simulation, yielding large gains on in-hand rotation and challenging tactile reorientation tasks.

institution_matches.title: RoboMME: Benchmarking and Understanding Memory for Robotic Generalist Policies
institution_matches.overview: Proposes RoboMME, a standardized benchmark of 16 manipulation tasks targeting temporal, spatial, object, and procedural memory in VLA models. Evaluates 14 memory-augmented variants of a π0.5-based policy, showing that memory design choices are highly task-dependent and exposing trade-offs for long-horizon manipulation.

institution_matches.title: Observing and Controlling Features in Vision-Language-Action Models
institution_matches.overview: Develops linear tools to observe and minimally intervene on internal features of VLAs such as π0.5 and OpenVLA. By steering latent representations with optimal-control-inspired linear interventions, the method reliably adjusts robot behavior online without fine-tuning, aligning actions with user preferences.

topic_matches.title: cuRoboV2: Dynamics-Aware Motion Generation with Depth-Fused Distance Fields for High-DoF Robots
topic_matches.overview: Presents a unified GPU-native stack for motion generation that couples B-spline trajectory optimization with fast TSDF/ESDF perception and scalable whole-body dynamics for manipulators and humanoids. Achieves high collision recall and large speedups over prior tools, enabling torque-limited, collision-free motion for up to 48-DoF systems.

topic_matches.title: UltraDexGrasp: Learning Universal Dexterous Grasping for Bimanual Robots with Synthetic Data
topic_matches.institution: Chinese University of Hong Kong; e Chinese University of Hong Kong
topic_matches.overview: Introduces an optimization- and planning-based pipeline for generating 20M frames of bimanual dexterous grasp trajectories across multiple strategies, forming the UltraDexGrasp-20M dataset. A point-cloud-based grasp policy trained purely in simulation achieves 81.2% success in zero-shot real-world dexterous grasping on novel objects.

topic_matches.title: Direct Contact-Tolerant Motion Planning With Vision Language Models
topic_matches.overview: Proposes a contact-tolerant planner that uses a VLM-driven point cloud partitioner to reason about where contact is permissible, forming a contact-aware point cloud. A neural controller then solves navigation as a perception-to-control optimization problem, enabling robust contact-rich motion in clutter on both simulated and real robots.

topic_matches.title: Risk-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Mobile Manipulation
topic_matches.overview: Learns risk-aware visuomotor policies for mobile manipulation by first training privileged distributional RL teachers with distortion risk metrics and then distilling them into depth-conditioned students. The resulting whole-body controllers exhibit adjustable risk sensitivity and improved worst-case performance in unmapped, dynamic environments.

topic_matches.title: Many-RRT*: Robust Joint-Space Trajectory Planning for Serial Manipulators
topic_matches.overview: Extends RRT*-Connect to plan simultaneously toward multiple IK solutions by growing parallel goal trees in joint space. This multi-goal strategy mitigates failures from poor IK choice, yielding substantially lower-cost trajectories and near-perfect success rates without increasing runtime.

topic_matches.title: GAIDE: Graph-based Attention Masking for Spatial- and Embodiment-aware Motion Planning
topic_matches.overview: Introduces GAIDE, a transformer-based neural informed sampler that encodes the spatial structure of the environment and robot embodiment as a graph with attention masking. Integrated with sampling-based planners for manipulators, GAIDE improves sample efficiency and planning success over uniform, hand-crafted, and prior learned samplers.

topic_matches.title: PhysiFlow: Physics-Aware Humanoid Whole-Body VLA via Multi-Brain Latent Flow Matching and Robust Tracking
topic_matches.overview: Proposes a multi-brain VLA framework that fuses semantic intent with physics-aware whole-body control for humanoids. Using latent flow matching and robust tracking, the system enables vision-language-guided, stable full-body coordination in dynamic tasks.

topic_matches.title: Omni-Manip: Beyond-FOV Large-Workspace Humanoid Manipulation with Omnidirectional 3D Perception
topic_matches.overview: Uses LiDAR-based panoramic point clouds and a Time-Aware Attention Pooling architecture to endow humanoids with 360° perception for manipulation. Trained via whole-body teleoperation data, the resulting visuomotor policy manipulates across large workspaces without frequent base motions, outperforming RGB-D-based baselines.

topic_matches.title: CT-Enabled Patient-Specific Simulation and Contact-Aware Robotic Planning for Cochlear Implantation
topic_matches.overview: Builds a CT-to-simulation pipeline that models electrode arrays as Cosserat rods with frictional contact in patient-specific cochlear anatomies. A differentiable equilibrium-constrained planner computes insertion directions that limit lateral forces and reduce locking/buckling risk, validated via simulations and benchtop experiments.

topic_matches.title: Curve-Induced Dynamical Systems on Riemannian Manifolds and Lie Groups
topic_matches.overview: Introduces CDSM, a method to construct stable dynamical systems directly on manifolds such as SE(3) and SPD(n) from nominal curves. Demonstrated on manipulators and a mobile manipulator, it enables real-time motion generation that adheres to geometric task structure while adapting online.
